learning_objectives: Understand the role of audio in player immersion and gameplay feedback
Identify the components of a game audio pipeline
Differentiate between linear and interactive audio
Recognize industry-standard audio formats and their trade-offs
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 1-2
Game Audio Programming Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Game Audio and Sound Design. Audio is often called the invisible half of the gaming experience -- it shapes emotion, reinforces mechanics, and grounds players in virtual worlds. Consider how different the opening minutes of a horror game feel when you mute the audio: the tension evaporates, jump scares become mundane, and the environment feels flat and lifeless. Sound designers craft every footstep, every ambient rustle, and every musical swell to manipulate the player's emotional state without them consciously noticing. In this course you will learn to create, implement, and mix interactive audio systems that respond dynamically to player actions. We begin by understanding the physics of sound and the digital audio pipeline that carries sound waves from a microphone or synthesizer through your DAW, into middleware, and finally out of the player's speakers or headphones at runtime. By semester's end you will have a professional demo reel showcasing original sound design, adaptive music, spatial audio, and middleware integration.
key_concepts: Properties of sound: frequency, amplitude, timbre, and envelope (ADSR)
Digital audio fundamentals: sample rate (44.1 kHz vs 48 kHz), bit depth (16-bit vs 24-bit), and codec formats (WAV, OGG, FLAC)
The game audio pipeline from asset creation to runtime playback
Differences between linear media audio (film, TV) and interactive game audio
Overview of professional game audio tools: DAWs (Reaper, Pro Tools), middleware (FMOD, Wwise), engines (Unity, Unreal)
Audio budgets in production: memory limits, voice count caps, and CPU overhead
The role of the audio team: sound designer, composer, audio programmer, technical sound designer
Perceptual audio encoding: how lossy compression exploits psychoacoustic masking to reduce file size
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparison of gameplay clips with and without audio; walkthrough of a DAW (Reaper) and its basic signal flow from input to master output
setup_requirements: Reaper DAW installed (free evaluation license)
Studio headphones or quality monitoring speakers
FMOD Studio installed
USB microphone or portable audio recorder

review.value: Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release -- the four phases that shape a sound's amplitude over time. Attack is the initial rise, Decay is the drop to sustain level, Sustain holds steady, and Release is the fade after the trigger ends.

Number of audio samples captured per second; 44100 Hz (CD quality) or 48000 Hz (professional standard for games and video). Higher rates capture more high-frequency detail but increase file size linearly.
Linear audio plays the same way every time (film scores). Interactive audio changes in response to player actions, game state, and environmental parameters in real time.
The phenomenon where louder sounds render nearby quieter sounds inaudible. Lossy codecs exploit this by removing masked frequencies the ear cannot perceive, reducing file size by 75-90%.

class_schedule.topic: Sound Effect Creation: Foley and Synthesis
learning_objectives: Record and edit Foley sound effects for common game actions
Design sound effects using subtractive and FM synthesis
Apply layering and processing techniques to create polished game sounds
Establish a consistent asset naming and organization workflow
readings: The Sound Effects Bible Ch. 3-5
Designing Sound by Andy Farnell Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Sound effects are the tactile feedback of games. Every button press, sword swing, footstep, and explosion communicates information to the player about the game world and their actions within it. Today we explore two core creation methods: recording real-world sounds (Foley) and generating them electronically (synthesis). Professional game audio combines both approaches. A single sword impact might layer a recorded metal clang with a synthesized low-frequency sub-bass boom and a processed cloth whoosh. Understanding both Foley and synthesis gives you the full creative palette. We will record sounds in class, process them through a signal chain of EQ, compression, and effects, and then create synthetic counterparts for comparison. The goal is to develop your ear for what makes a game sound satisfying -- the weight, the responsiveness, the tactile quality that makes virtual actions feel physically real.
key_concepts: Foley recording technique: microphone selection (cardioid, shotgun), room treatment, performance technique
Common Foley categories in games: footsteps, impacts, cloth, UI feedback, environmental interactions
Subtractive synthesis: oscillators (saw, square, noise), filters (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass), and envelopes for SFX
FM synthesis for metallic, bell-like, and sci-fi sound design using operator/carrier relationships
Layering multiple sources and processing with EQ, compression, and reverb to create composite sounds
Gain staging: maintaining proper signal levels through the recording and processing chain to prevent clipping
Batch processing workflows for creating multiple variations from a single source recording
File naming conventions and folder organization for game audio asset libraries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live Foley recording session for footstep and weapon impact sounds; synthesizing a laser blast in Vital synthesizer; layering both approaches for a hybrid sci-fi impact
setup_requirements: Portable audio recorder or USB microphone
Vital synthesizer (free) installed
Reaper project template from course resources
Various Foley props (shoes, metal objects, fabric)

review.value: The art of recording real-world sounds to replace or enhance audio in post-production, named after sound artist Jack Foley who pioneered live sound effect performance for film.
Starting with a harmonically rich waveform (sawtooth, square) and sculpting it by removing frequencies with filters, shaped by amplitude and filter envelopes.
Maintaining optimal signal levels at each point in the audio chain. Recording at -12 to -6 dBFS peak leaves headroom for processing without clipping.
Combining multiple sound sources (transient, body, tail, sub-bass) into a single composite effect. Each layer contributes a different frequency range or temporal characteristic.

learning_objectives: Design music that responds dynamically to gameplay state changes
Implement horizontal and vertical re-sequencing techniques
Build a layered adaptive music system in FMOD Studio
Compose musical material that maintains coherence across adaptive transitions
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 7-9
FMOD Studio documentation: Music system overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Unlike film scores that play linearly from start to finish, game music must react to unpredictable player actions. A player might spend thirty seconds in exploration before triggering combat, or they might linger for ten minutes collecting items. The music must adapt seamlessly to these unpredictable timelines without jarring transitions or awkward loops. Adaptive music systems solve this problem through two fundamental techniques: horizontal re-sequencing (branching between different musical sections) and vertical remixing (adding or removing instrument layers within a single piece). The most sophisticated games combine both approaches with parameter-driven real-time mixing. Today we build a three-intensity combat music system in FMOD Studio, starting from composed stems and ending with smooth parameter-driven crossfade transitions that a game programmer can trigger with a single API call. You will learn to think like both a composer and a systems designer, crafting musical material specifically for interactive behavior.
key_concepts: Horizontal re-sequencing: branching between musical segments at transition points based on game events
Vertical remixing: adding or removing instrument layers based on game state intensity parameters
Stinger systems for one-shot musical events tied to gameplay moments (victory fanfare, death sting, discovery)
Tempo and key synchronization across adaptive segments to ensure harmonic coherence during transitions
FMOD music timeline: regions, transition markers, quantization settings, and parameter-driven logic
Composing for adaptivity: writing stems that work in any combination, using shared harmonic progressions
Beat-synced transitions vs free transitions: when musical timing matters and when immediate response matters
Memory considerations: streaming vs pre-loaded stems, and the trade-off between musical variety and RAM usage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a three-intensity combat music system in FMOD Studio with smooth crossfade transitions driven by a single game parameter (0 = exploration, 0.5 = tension, 1.0 = combat)
setup_requirements: FMOD Studio 2.02+
Provided music stems (exploration, tension, combat layers)
Unity FMOD integration plugin
Musical notation or piano reference for key/tempo alignment

review.value: Switching between different musical sections or branches at defined transition points based on game events. Best for distinct state changes like peaceful-to-combat.
Layering or muting individual instrument tracks within a single musical piece to change intensity without changing harmonic structure. Best for gradual shifts.
Musical transitions that wait for the next bar or beat boundary before switching, ensuring the new section begins on a musically logical moment.
A short, one-shot musical phrase triggered by a specific game event that plays over the current music, such as a fanfare when collecting a key item.

learning_objectives: Select appropriate microphone types and polar patterns for different recording scenarios
Configure recording environments to minimize noise and optimize signal quality
Apply professional editing and cleanup techniques to raw recordings
Build a reusable game audio asset library with consistent quality standards
readings: The Sound Effects Bible Ch. 6-8
Game Audio Programming Ch. 2 - Audio Asset Creation

lecture_notes.introduction: The quality of your raw recordings determines the ceiling for your finished sound effects. No amount of processing can fix a poorly recorded source -- clipping, room reflections, handling noise, and electrical hum are all enemies of clean game audio. Professional sound designers invest heavily in recording technique because clean source material gives them maximum flexibility in post-production. Today we examine the entire recording chain from microphone selection through analog-to-digital conversion to file management. We cover field recording for environmental ambiences, studio Foley for character sounds, and contact microphones for unusual textures. You will learn that the most important tool is not the most expensive microphone but the understanding of how placement, polar pattern, and gain structure interact to capture the sound you actually want. We also cover editing workflows that transform raw recordings into game-ready assets: trimming silence, removing noise floors, normalizing levels, and creating consistent sets of variations for randomized playback in middleware.
key_concepts: Microphone types: condenser (high sensitivity, studio use), dynamic (rugged, loud sources), ribbon (warm, fragile), contact (vibration pickup)
Polar patterns: cardioid (front-focused), omnidirectional (all directions), figure-8 (front and back), shotgun (narrow beam)
Signal chain: microphone -> preamp (gain) -> A/D converter -> recording device, with each link affecting noise and quality
Room acoustics: reflection, absorption, and diffusion and how they color recordings
Field recording: capturing environmental ambiences, weather, traffic, nature with portable equipment
Noise reduction techniques: spectral editing, noise gates, expansion, and the danger of over-processing
Batch editing workflows: strip silence, normalize, fade, rename, and export dozens of variations efficiently
Metadata and file management: iXML metadata tags, consistent naming schemes, backup strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative recording session using three microphone types on the same sound source, followed by spectral noise removal in iZotope RX and batch export of game-ready assets
setup_requirements: Multiple microphone types available in lab studio
iZotope RX (trial or edu license) for spectral editing
Reaper DAW with batch processing actions
Portable audio recorder for field recording exercises

review.value: A heart-shaped pickup pattern that is most sensitive to sound arriving from the front and rejects sound from the rear. The standard choice for Foley and voiceover recording.
The difference in decibels between the desired signal and the noise floor. Higher SNR means cleaner recordings. Proper gain staging maximizes SNR throughout the chain.
Visualizing audio as a time-frequency spectrogram and selectively removing unwanted sounds (clicks, hum, room tone) without affecting the desired signal.
Adjusting the peak or perceived loudness of an audio file to a target level, ensuring all assets in a library play at consistent volumes when triggered in-game.

learning_objectives: Implement 3D spatialization for sound sources in a game engine
Configure attenuation curves and occlusion for realistic sound propagation
Apply HRTF-based binaural rendering for headphone playback
Design reverb zones that match environmental acoustics
readings: Game Audio Programming Ch. 5-6
FMOD documentation: 3D Positioning and Spatialization

lecture_notes.introduction: Spatial audio transforms flat stereo mixes into immersive three-dimensional soundscapes. In the physical world, your brain constantly uses audio cues to build a spatial model of your environment. Sounds arrive at different times and volumes to each ear, they are filtered by the shape of your outer ear, and they reflect off surfaces before reaching you. Accurate spatial audio in games provides players with critical gameplay information -- the direction of approaching footsteps, the distance of an explosion, whether an enemy is above or below -- while deepening the sense of physical presence in the virtual world. Today we implement a complete spatial audio system in Unity with FMOD, covering 3D positioning, distance attenuation, occlusion and obstruction filtering, and environmental reverb zones. We compare panning-based spatialization with HRTF binaural rendering and discuss when each approach is appropriate. By session's end you will have a Unity scene where closing a door audibly muffles sounds behind it and walking into a cave changes the reverb character of every sound.
key_concepts: Sound attenuation models: linear, logarithmic, and custom rolloff curves defining volume decrease over distance
Occlusion and obstruction: simulating sound blocked by geometry using low-pass filtering and volume reduction
HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) for binaural 3D positioning using personalized ear-shape filtering
Reverb zones and environmental acoustics simulation: room size, decay time, early reflections
Ambisonics and channel-based surround sound formats (5.1, 7.1, Atmos) for speaker-based playback
Doppler effect simulation: pitch shifting sounds as source and listener move relative to each other
Listener orientation: how the camera or player head rotation affects perceived sound direction
Performance budgets for spatial audio: CPU cost of HRTF convolution vs simple panning per voice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring a Unity scene with FMOD 3D spatializer, custom attenuation curves, occlusion raycast system, and reverb zone transitions from outdoor to indoor spaces
setup_requirements: Unity project with FMOD integration
Headphones required for binaural demonstrations
Provided 3D test scene with multiple sound emitters
FMOD Resonance Audio plugin for HRTF processing

review.value: The decrease in sound volume as distance increases between the listener and the source, modeled by rolloff curves. Logarithmic rolloff mimics real-world inverse-square law behavior.
Head-Related Transfer Function -- models how human ears perceive sound direction through frequency filtering and inter-aural time/level differences. Requires headphones for accurate reproduction.
Occlusion: sound source and listener are fully separated by geometry (wall between them). Obstruction: direct path is blocked but indirect paths exist (sound goes around a pillar).
A trigger volume in the game world that applies specific reverb parameters (room size, decay, reflections) to all sounds playing within it, simulating the acoustics of that space.

class_schedule.topic: Audio Middleware: FMOD and Wwise in Production
learning_objectives: Architect a complete game audio project using FMOD or Wwise
Implement parameter-driven sound events with randomization and variation
Profile audio CPU and memory usage and apply optimization strategies
Design sound bank organization for efficient streaming and loading
readings: FMOD Studio User Manual: Events and Parameters
Wwise Fundamentals documentation
Game Audio Programming Ch. 8 - Audio Middleware Integration

lecture_notes.introduction: Audio middleware bridges the gap between sound designers and programmers, allowing complex, data-driven audio behaviors without recompiling game code. FMOD and Wwise are the industry standard tools used in productions ranging from indie titles to AAA franchises with tens of thousands of audio events. The core value proposition is separation of concerns: sound designers author behavior in the middleware tool (volume curves, randomization, parameter responses, mixing), while programmers simply trigger events and set parameters through a lightweight API. This decoupling means audio iteration can happen independently of code changes -- a sound designer can completely redesign how a weapon sounds without a programmer touching a single line. Today we architect a vehicle engine audio system that responds to RPM and throttle load parameters, demonstrating how middleware enables complex real-time audio behavior that would require thousands of lines of custom code to replicate. We also cover the practical production concerns of sound bank management, memory budgets, and CPU profiling that determine whether your beautiful audio design actually ships.
key_concepts: Event-based audio architecture: decoupling sound design from code through named events with internal complexity hidden from programmers
Game parameters driving real-time audio changes: RPM, health, distance, speed, weather intensity mapped to volume, pitch, filter, and crossfade
Randomization containers for variation: multi-instrument with random or shuffle selection, pitch and volume randomization ranges
Sound banks: packaging audio assets into loadable units organized by level, area, or category for memory management
Streaming vs in-memory: long sounds (music, ambience, dialogue) stream from disk while short sounds (SFX) load into RAM
Profiling tools: real-time voice count, CPU percentage, memory allocation, and peak bandwidth monitoring
Snapshot and mixing: real-time mix state presets for different game contexts (menu, gameplay, cutscene, pause)
Middleware comparison: FMOD timeline-based workflow vs Wwise actor-mixer hierarchy and interactive music system
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a vehicle engine audio system in FMOD with RPM and load parameters driving pitch, crossfade between engine layers, and conditional exhaust pops during throttle release
setup_requirements: FMOD Studio 2.02+ with Unity integration
Provided vehicle audio assets (engine layers at multiple RPM points, exhaust samples)
Unity profiler for audio performance monitoring
Wwise free authoring tool for comparison exercise

review.value: A self-contained sound behavior definition in middleware that can be triggered by game code without knowing its internal structure. Events encapsulate randomization, parameters, mixing, and effects.
A compiled binary package of audio assets and event metadata loaded at runtime. Banks are organized by game area or category to control memory usage -- only load what the player needs.
A named floating-point value set by game code that drives audio behavior in middleware. Parameters map to volume, pitch, filter frequency, and crossfade position through designer-defined curves.
Capping the maximum number of simultaneously playing sounds to prevent CPU overload. Lower-priority voices are stolen or virtualized when the limit is reached.

class_schedule.topic: Voice Acting Direction and Dialogue Systems
learning_objectives: Direct voice acting sessions that produce consistent, high-quality performances
Design and implement a branching dialogue audio system with priority management
Apply dialogue mixing techniques including ducking and sidechain compression
Plan dialogue recording sessions with efficient scheduling and file management
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 12
GDC Vault: 'Dialogue Systems in Games' lecture notes
Game Audio Programming Ch. 10 - Dialogue and Localization

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice acting brings characters to life and is often the most expensive audio production element in game development. A single AAA RPG may contain over 100,000 lines of recorded dialogue across dozens of characters and multiple languages. The technical systems that deliver those performances -- triggering the right line at the right time, managing priority when multiple characters speak simultaneously, ducking music and ambience to ensure speech intelligibility -- are as important as the performances themselves. Today we cover both the creative and technical sides of game dialogue. On the creative side, we practice voice direction techniques that help actors deliver performances consistent with character and context. On the technical side, we implement a dialogue priority system in FMOD that handles simultaneous speech requests, automatic ducking of background audio, and subtitle synchronization. We also discuss the production logistics of recording sessions: casting, scheduling, file naming for thousands of lines, and planning for localization into multiple languages.
key_concepts: Casting and audition processes for game voice talent: character briefs, audition sides, callback evaluation
Directing techniques: providing context without overexplaining, reference delivery, emotional range bracketing
Dialogue management: triggering, queuing, priority levels, interruption rules for simultaneous speech
Dialogue ducking: automatically lowering music and ambient audio when speech plays to ensure intelligibility
Sidechain compression for dialogue: compressing music bus keyed from dialogue bus for transparent level reduction
Line naming conventions for large projects: character_context_emotion_take (e.g., NPC_GUARD_GREETING_NEUTRAL_01)
Localization planning: consistent line IDs, timing slack for longer languages, lip-sync considerations
Barks and callouts: short contextual voice lines triggered by gameplay events (combat, detection, damage)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock voice direction session with provided script; implementing a three-priority dialogue system in FMOD with automatic ducking and queue management
setup_requirements: FMOD Studio project from previous sessions
Provided dialogue script and placeholder voice recordings
Monitoring headphones for critical listening
Recording setup for in-class direction exercise

review.value: Automatically lowering music and ambient audio levels when dialogue plays to ensure speech intelligibility. Typically -6 to -12 dB reduction with 100-200ms attack and 500ms release.
Assigning importance levels to dialogue types (critical story > quest info > ambient chatter > barks) so higher-priority lines interrupt or queue ahead of lower-priority ones.
A short, context-sensitive voice line triggered by gameplay events. Barks use randomized selection from pools of variations to prevent repetition fatigue during extended play.
The process of recording dialogue in multiple languages using consistent line IDs, timing references, and character context documents so foreign-language performances match the original intent.

learning_objectives: Generate sound effects procedurally using real-time synthesis techniques
Implement parameter-driven procedural audio for dynamic game environments
Compare procedural audio with sample-based approaches for various use cases
Use visual patching tools for rapid procedural audio prototyping
readings: Designing Sound by Andy Farnell Ch. 10-15
Game Audio Programming Ch. 12 - Procedural Audio
Pure Data documentation: Audio synthesis basics

lecture_notes.introduction: Procedural audio generates sound algorithmically at runtime rather than playing back pre-recorded samples. This approach offers compelling advantages for game development: infinite variation without consuming memory for stored samples, sounds that respond continuously to game parameters rather than triggering discrete clips, and the ability to create physically accurate acoustic models of objects and environments. A procedural wind system does not loop a recording -- it synthesizes turbulence, pitch variation, and gusting in real time based on weather parameters. A procedural engine does not crossfade between RPM samples -- it models cylinder firing, exhaust resonance, and intake noise from physical principles. The trade-off is CPU cost and the difficulty of achieving photorealistic quality for organic sounds. Today we build several procedural audio systems: wind, fire, footsteps on procedural terrain, and UI feedback tones. We use Pure Data patches for prototyping and then discuss strategies for porting procedural designs to production middleware or custom engine code. By session's end you will understand when procedural audio is the right choice and when samples remain superior.
key_concepts: Physical modeling synthesis: simulating acoustic properties of real objects (strings, tubes, membranes) mathematically
Granular synthesis: decomposing sound into tiny grains and recombining them with randomized parameters for texture generation
Noise-based synthesis: shaping white/pink/brown noise with filters and envelopes for wind, rain, fire, and ocean sounds
Parameter mapping: connecting game state variables (wind speed, surface material, object mass) directly to synthesis controls
Pure Data (Pd) as a prototyping environment for procedural audio with visual patching
CPU budgeting: procedural audio trades memory for computation, requiring careful profiling on target hardware
Hybrid approaches: combining procedural base layers with sample-based transients for best quality and variety
Procedural music: algorithmic composition techniques for ambient, generative, and interactive musical systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a procedural wind system in Pure Data that responds to a speed parameter (0-100 km/h) controlling noise filtering, pitch modulation, and gusting intensity patterns
setup_requirements: Pure Data (Pd) installed for visual audio patching
FMOD Studio with programmer instrument for Pd integration concepts
Unity project for testing real-time parameter control
Audio profiler for measuring CPU cost of procedural vs sample-based systems

review.value: Sound generated algorithmically at runtime rather than from recorded samples, enabling infinite variation, continuous parameter response, and reduced memory usage at the cost of CPU time.
Breaking sound into tiny grains (10-50ms) and playing them back with randomized pitch, timing, and amplitude to create evolving textures from a small amount of source material.
Simulating the physics of sound production (vibrating strings, air columns, struck surfaces) mathematically to generate realistic acoustic behavior responsive to input parameters.
Combining procedural base layers (continuous textures) with sample-based elements (sharp transients, complex organic sounds) to leverage the strengths of both methods.

learning_objectives: Compose original music that serves specific gameplay functions and emotional contexts
Write adaptive-ready musical material with modular structure for interactive systems
Apply instrumentation and orchestration choices appropriate to game genre and setting
Deliver music assets in production-ready formats with proper documentation
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 3-6
Video Game Music Composition (Winifred Phillips), selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Game music composition requires a unique mindset that blends creative artistry with systems thinking. Unlike a film composer who writes to a locked picture, a game composer writes music that must function across wildly variable timelines and player experiences. Your exploration theme might play for thirty seconds or thirty minutes. Your combat music must transition seamlessly from a minor skirmish to a boss fight. Your menu theme must loop without listener fatigue. These constraints demand specific compositional techniques: modular song structures, harmonic progressions that support seamless looping, stem-based arrangements where any combination of layers sounds musically complete, and emotional arcs that function at any point in the timeline. Today we compose a complete three-state adaptive music cue from scratch. We begin with a harmonic progression and melodic motif, arrange it for three intensity levels, export individual stems, and prepare everything for integration into FMOD. You will leave with both the creative skills to write compelling game music and the technical understanding to make it work in an interactive system.
key_concepts: Functional music categories: menu/title, exploration, tension, combat, victory, defeat, cutscene, ambient
Looping techniques: seamless loops using crossfade tails, rhythmic and harmonic alignment at loop boundaries
Stem-based composition: writing arrangements where subsets of instruments form complete musical statements
Leitmotif and thematic development: associating melodic themes with characters, locations, or narrative arcs
Genre-appropriate instrumentation: orchestral for fantasy, synthesizers for sci-fi, acoustic for indie, hybrid for action
Dynamic range and loudness: composing with headroom for ducking, targeting -14 to -16 LUFS for game music
Tempo and time signature considerations: BPM choices that align with gameplay pacing and transition quantization
Music asset delivery: file formats, naming conventions, cue sheets, and composer documentation packages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Composing a three-layer adaptive exploration cue in a DAW: bass/harmony foundation, melodic layer, and percussive energy layer, each functioning independently and together
setup_requirements: DAW with virtual instruments (Reaper + provided VST plugins)
MIDI keyboard or piano for composition
FMOD Studio for stem integration testing
Reference tracks from provided game music playlist

review.value: A music file where the ending transitions perfectly back to the beginning with no audible click, gap, or rhythmic discontinuity. Achieved by aligning the final beat with the first and crossfading the tail.
An individual layer of a musical arrangement exported as a separate audio file. A full cue might have stems for strings, brass, percussion, and synths that the middleware mixes in real time.
A recurring musical theme associated with a character, place, or idea. When the motif appears, it triggers the listener's association with that element even in a new context.
Loudness Units relative to Full Scale -- a perceptual loudness measurement standard. Game music typically targets -14 to -16 LUFS to leave dynamic headroom for SFX and dialogue.

learning_objectives: Write game engine code that triggers and controls audio events through middleware APIs
Implement runtime audio behaviors: footstep surface detection, impact velocity scaling, environmental audio zones
Debug audio integration issues using middleware profiling and engine logging
Design audio manager architectures that centralize audio control in game code
readings: Game Audio Programming Ch. 7-9
FMOD Unity API Documentation: Studio Event Emitter, Runtime Manager
Unity Audio Documentation: AudioSource, AudioListener, AudioMixer

lecture_notes.introduction: The most beautifully designed sounds and music are worthless if they do not play correctly in the game. Audio integration is the bridge between the sound designer's FMOD project and the player's ears, and it requires both programming skill and audio sensibility. A technical sound designer or audio programmer must understand how to trigger events at the right moments, pass game state to audio parameters, manage audio resource lifecycle, and debug the inevitable issues where sounds play when they should not or fail to play when they should. Today we write the integration code for a complete game level. We implement a surface detection system that plays appropriate footstep sounds based on physics material, a velocity-based impact system that scales volume and selects sound variants based on collision force, and an environmental audio zone system that blends ambient soundscapes and reverb settings as the player moves through the world. We build an audio manager singleton that provides a clean API for game designers and programmers to interact with the audio system without needing to understand middleware internals.
key_concepts: FMOD Unity API: creating event instances, setting parameters, starting/stopping events, releasing resources
Surface-based audio: using physics materials or terrain textures to select footstep and impact sound variants
Velocity-to-audio mapping: converting collision force into volume, pitch variation, and sound selection for impacts
Audio zone system: trigger volumes that blend ambient soundscapes and reverb snapshots with crossfade transitions
Audio manager pattern: centralized singleton providing high-level audio API to gameplay code
Object pooling for audio: reusing event instances to avoid allocation and garbage collection during gameplay
Coroutine and callback patterns: handling audio events that must wait for gameplay timing (music sync, dialogue sequences)
Debugging techniques: FMOD live update for remote profiling, logging event triggers, visualizing emitter positions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a complete footstep audio system in Unity: raycast surface detection, FMOD parameter-driven material selection, velocity-scaled volume, and left/right foot alternation
setup_requirements: Unity project with FMOD integration and character controller
FMOD project with footstep, impact, and ambient events configured
Multiple terrain types with physics materials assigned
FMOD Live Update enabled for real-time debugging

review.value: A runtime handle to a playing sound in FMOD. You create an instance from an event description, set parameters, start playback, and release it when finished. Instance lifetime must be managed to avoid memory leaks.
Using raycasts or collision data to determine what material the player is standing on or hitting, then passing that information to the audio system to select appropriate sound variants.
A centralized code component that wraps middleware API calls into a game-specific interface, handling event caching, parameter management, and resource cleanup in one place.
FMOD's ability to connect to a running game in real time, allowing sound designers to adjust volumes, parameters, and effects while the game is playing for rapid iteration.

learning_objectives: Build a game audio mix bus structure with proper routing and grouping
Apply dynamic mixing techniques that respond to gameplay context
Master final audio output to meet platform-specific loudness standards
Create mix snapshots for different game states (menu, gameplay, cutscene, pause)
readings: Game Audio Programming Ch. 14 - Mixing and Output
Mixing Audio: Concepts, Practices, and Tools (Izhaki) Ch. 1-4
EBU R128 Loudness Recommendation documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Mixing is where all the individual audio elements -- sound effects, music, dialogue, ambience -- come together into a cohesive whole. A great mix is invisible: the player hears a unified soundscape where every element is audible at the right moment and nothing fights for attention. A poor mix is immediately noticeable: music drowning out dialogue, footsteps louder than explosions, UI sounds piercing through combat chaos. Game mixing is fundamentally different from film or music mixing because the mix is not fixed -- it must adapt in real time to unpredictable gameplay. When a boss battle erupts, combat SFX need spectral space that requires music to duck and ambient sounds to attenuate. When the player opens a menu during combat, the entire soundscape must smoothly transition to a muffled, quieted state. Today we build a complete mix bus structure in FMOD, define mix snapshots for each game state, and implement dynamic mixing rules that ensure clarity across all gameplay scenarios. We also cover loudness standards (LUFS) and platform-specific requirements for submission to console manufacturers and storefronts.
key_concepts: Bus routing hierarchy: master -> music, SFX, dialogue, ambience, UI with sub-buses for fine control
Frequency separation: ensuring each audio category occupies its own spectral space to prevent masking conflicts
Dynamic mixing: automated level adjustments based on gameplay state (combat music up, ambience down during battle)
Mix snapshots: preset mix states (menu, gameplay, cutscene, pause) with smooth transition parameters between them
Sidechain ducking: using dialogue presence to automatically reduce music and ambience levels for speech clarity
Loudness standards: EBU R128 (-23 LUFS) for broadcast, -14 to -16 LUFS for game masters, platform-specific requirements
Dynamic range management: compression and limiting on master bus versus per-bus processing strategies
Platform considerations: TV speakers vs headphones vs mobile speakers and how to optimize the mix for each output
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete FMOD mixer with bus hierarchy, implementing gameplay-to-cutscene snapshot transitions, and measuring output loudness with LUFS metering
setup_requirements: FMOD Studio project with all course audio assets integrated
LUFS loudness meter plugin (Youlean Loudness Meter free)
Unity project for testing dynamic mix transitions
Multiple output devices for testing (headphones, speakers, phone speaker)

review.value: The tree structure of audio routing groups in a mixer. All sound effects route to an SFX bus, all music to a music bus, etc., with each bus having independent volume, EQ, and effects before summing to the master.
A saved mixer state that can be blended to during gameplay. A 'pause' snapshot might reduce music by -6 dB and apply a low-pass filter to all SFX, with a 300ms transition time.
Loudness Units relative to Full Scale, measured using the ITU-R BS.1770 algorithm. Unlike peak metering, LUFS measures perceived loudness over time, matching human hearing perception.
When two sounds occupy the same frequency range simultaneously, the louder one makes the quieter one inaudible. Good mixing separates elements into complementary frequency bands.

learning_objectives: Profile and optimize audio CPU, memory, and bandwidth usage for target platforms
Implement audio LOD (level of detail) systems that scale quality with distance and priority
Configure platform-specific audio settings for PC, console, and mobile deployment
Manage audio asset compression and streaming for different storage constraints
readings: Game Audio Programming Ch. 15-16 - Optimization and Platform Audio
FMOD Studio User Manual: Profiling and Optimization
Console Technical Requirement Checklists (provided excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your beautifully crafted audio design must fit within the hardware constraints of your target platform. A mobile phone has a fraction of the memory and CPU budget of a gaming PC, yet players on both platforms expect rich, immersive audio. Optimization is not about making audio worse -- it is about making intelligent trade-offs that preserve the player's perception of quality while meeting strict technical budgets. Today we learn the practical techniques that ship audio on real hardware. We profile an over-budget audio system, identify the most expensive elements, and apply targeted optimizations: reducing voice counts with priority-based stealing, implementing audio LOD that simplifies distant sounds, selecting appropriate compression codecs for different asset types, and configuring streaming versus in-memory loading for optimal bandwidth usage. We also cover platform-specific requirements: console certification checklists that mandate audio behavior during suspend/resume and controller disconnect, mobile backgrounding rules, and web audio API limitations for browser games.
key_concepts: Audio profiling metrics: voice count, CPU percentage, memory allocation, disk bandwidth, and how to measure each
Voice management: priority-based voice stealing, virtualization of inaudible voices, maximum voice count caps
Audio LOD: reducing spatialization quality, disabling effects, and simplifying events for distant or low-priority sounds
Compression codec selection: Vorbis for music/ambience (quality), ADPCM for short SFX (decode speed), PCM for critical sounds
Streaming configuration: buffer sizes, read-ahead, and the trade-off between memory savings and seek latency
Memory pooling: pre-allocating audio memory to avoid runtime allocation and fragmentation
Platform certification: console TRC/TCR requirements for audio (suspend/resume, mute behavior, output format detection)
Cross-platform audio strategy: scaling quality settings (low/medium/high) based on detected hardware capability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Profiling an over-budget FMOD project in Unity: identifying the top memory consumers, reducing voice count from 128 to 48 with priority-based stealing, and measuring the before/after impact on CPU and memory
setup_requirements: Unity project with intentionally over-budget audio for optimization exercise
FMOD Studio profiler connected via Live Update
Unity Profiler with audio detail enabled
Platform-specific build targets configured (mobile, desktop)

review.value: When the maximum voice count is reached, the system stops the lowest-priority, least-audible voice to make room for a new higher-priority sound. Players should never notice voices being stolen.

Level of Detail for audio: reducing processing complexity for sounds that are far from the listener, inaudible, or low-priority. Saves CPU by skipping spatialization and effects on sounds the player will not notice.
Vorbis: best quality per byte but expensive to decode. ADPCM: moderate quality, very fast decode, 4:1 compression. PCM: no quality loss, no decode cost, largest file size.
The disk bandwidth available for audio streaming, typically 2-4 MB/s on consoles. Music and dialogue stream from disk while SFX load into memory, balancing memory and bandwidth constraints.

class_schedule.topic: Sound Design for Specific Game Genres
learning_objectives: Identify genre-specific audio conventions and player expectations for major game genres
Design audio systems tailored to the unique requirements of FPS, RPG, horror, racing, and mobile games
Analyze published games to reverse-engineer their audio design decisions
Adapt sound design principles to emerging genres and platforms including VR and AR
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 10-11 (Genre-Specific Approaches)
GDC Vault: genre-specific audio talks (provided playlist)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every game genre has evolved its own audio language -- a set of conventions that players expect and that designers can leverage or subvert for creative effect. An FPS player expects precise spatial audio that communicates threat direction. An RPG player expects lush environmental ambiences that establish world-building. A horror player expects sound design that manipulates psychological tension through silence, subtlety, and sudden disruption. A racing player expects engine audio that communicates speed and mechanical feedback through pitch, volume, and distortion. Understanding these conventions is essential for two reasons: first, meeting player expectations establishes the baseline of competent audio design; second, knowing the conventions allows you to break them intentionally for creative impact. Today we analyze the audio design of published games across five major genres, reverse-engineering the systems and techniques that make their audio effective. We then discuss emerging platforms -- VR, AR, and spatial computing -- where audio conventions are still being established and designers have the opportunity to define the standards.
key_concepts: FPS audio: precise spatialization for competitive advantage, weapon punch and impact feedback, footstep material systems
RPG audio: rich ambient soundscapes, extensive dialogue systems, adaptive music for exploration and combat phases
Horror audio: psychological tension through silence and low-frequency rumble, diegetic sound sources, unreliable audio cues
Racing audio: engine audio as primary player feedback, Doppler effects, tire squeal and surface interaction systems
Mobile audio: compressed asset budgets, speaker limitations, play-with-sound-off design, haptic feedback integration
VR audio requirements: head-tracked spatial audio, near-field interaction sounds, comfort considerations for audio latency
Audio accessibility: subtitles, visual sound indicators, mono downmix compatibility, volume normalization
Genre subversion: using audio expectation violations for narrative or emotional effect (horror silence, comedy mismatch)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative audio analysis of four published games (one per genre): identifying audio systems, parameter mappings, and design decisions through active listening and middleware reconstruction
setup_requirements: Access to published games for analysis (provided list with timestamps)
Audio analysis worksheet with genre-specific evaluation criteria
Recording setup for capturing game audio examples
Headphones for critical listening sessions

review.value: Sound whose source exists within the game world (a radio playing in a room, an NPC talking). Contrasts with non-diegetic sound (background music score, UI feedback) that has no in-world source.
Design practices that ensure game audio information is available to players with hearing impairments: visual sound indicators, subtitle support, haptic feedback alternatives, and mono compatibility.
Head-tracked binaural audio that updates spatialization in real time as the player looks around. Requires low-latency HRTF processing to prevent audio-visual mismatch that causes discomfort.
The cycle where player actions produce sounds that inform subsequent player decisions. Tight, responsive audio feedback makes controls feel precise and the game world feel reactive.

class_schedule.topic: Professional Audio Workflows and Demo Reel Production
learning_objectives: Organize a professional game audio production pipeline from pre-production to delivery
Create a demo reel that effectively showcases game audio skills to potential employers
Build a portfolio presentation that communicates both creative and technical abilities
Navigate the game audio job market and understand career paths in game audio
readings: Game Audio Career Guide (IGDA Game Audio SIG)
Portfolio and Demo Reel Best Practices (provided industry guides)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your demo reel is the single most important tool in your game audio career. It is what hiring managers watch before deciding whether to interview you, and it must communicate your skills within the first thirty seconds. Today we focus on the professional practices that will carry you from student to working game audio professional. We cover production pipeline organization -- how professional studios manage thousands of audio assets across multi-year projects with version control, naming conventions, and approval workflows. We then focus entirely on demo reel creation: selecting your strongest work, editing video that showcases audio effectively (before/after comparisons, isolated element breakdowns, split-screen with middleware visualization), and structuring the reel to frontload your most impressive work. We also discuss the game audio job market: the difference between in-house audio teams and outsourcing studios, freelance versus full-time employment, salary expectations, and the networking strategies that actually land jobs in this competitive field.
key_concepts: Production pipeline: pre-production audio design docs, production asset tracking, integration milestones, final mix and delivery
Version control for audio: naming conventions with revision numbers, change logs, approval workflows between audio and design teams
Demo reel structure: 2-3 minutes maximum, strongest work first, before/after comparisons, clear labeling of your contribution
Video editing for audio showcase: avoiding visual distractions, ensuring audio is the focus, including middleware visualizations
Portfolio website: embedded reel, project case studies with technical breakdowns, about/contact, tool proficiency list
Game audio career paths: sound designer, composer, audio programmer, technical sound designer, audio director
Networking: GDC Audio track, Game Audio Network Guild (GANG), social media presence, game jam participation
Contract and freelance practices: rate negotiation, project scoping, deliverable definitions, intellectual property agreements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live editing session: selecting and arranging clips from course projects into a demo reel structure, adding before/after comparison segments, and rendering at portfolio-ready quality
setup_requirements: Video editing software (DaVinci Resolve free or similar)
All course project files and recordings compiled
Screen recording of middleware sessions for reel inclusion
Portfolio website template or hosting account

review.value: A hiring manager decides within 30 seconds whether to keep watching. Your reel must open with your absolute best work and immediately demonstrate your primary skill (sound design, composition, or implementation).
The most effective demo reel technique: showing gameplay with original audio, then the same footage with your redesigned audio. Clearly communicates the value and quality of your work.
A portfolio section that shows your middleware project, parameter mappings, and implementation code alongside the final audio result. Demonstrates both creative and technical competence.
Game Audio Network Guild -- the primary professional organization for game audio professionals. Annual awards, job board, mentorship programs, and community forums.

class_schedule.topic: Final Project Presentations and Industry Retrospective
learning_objectives: Present a completed game audio project to peers and evaluators in a professional format
Provide constructive critique of peer work using industry evaluation standards
Reflect on the semester's learning and identify areas for continued professional development
Connect course skills to current industry trends and emerging opportunities
readings: Review all course materials for comprehensive reflection
GDC 2024 Audio Track summaries (provided reading list)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is your opportunity to present your capstone demo reel to the class and receive feedback from peers and the instructor in a format that mirrors industry portfolio reviews. Each student will present their demo reel followed by a brief technical Q&A. Beyond the presentations, we will take stock of what you have learned over the past fifteen weeks and look ahead to where game audio is heading. The game audio field is evolving rapidly: machine learning is being applied to procedural audio generation, spatial computing platforms demand new approaches to immersive sound design, and real-time ray-traced acoustics are moving from research to production. The technical foundations you have built in this course -- signal processing, middleware architecture, spatial audio, adaptive systems, and integration programming -- will serve as the base for engaging with these emerging technologies. We close with a retrospective discussion about the industry, career paths, and each student's plan for continuing their game audio development beyond this course.
key_concepts: Professional presentation skills: structuring a demo reel walkthrough, anticipating technical questions, speaking to creative intent
Peer critique methodology: specific, actionable feedback focused on craft rather than personal taste
Industry trends: machine learning for audio, real-time ray-traced acoustics, spatial computing audio requirements
Ambisonics and object-based audio for next-generation spatial formats (Dolby Atmos, Sony 360 Reality Audio)
AI-assisted audio tools: text-to-SFX generation, intelligent audio repair, automated mixing assistance
Continued professional development: certifications (Wwise, FMOD), game jam participation, open-source contributions
Building a sustainable career: diversifying skills, maintaining a current portfolio, staying connected to the community
Post-mortem analysis: what worked, what would you do differently, and what you learned about your creative process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student demo reel presentations with live Q&A followed by group discussion of industry trends and career planning
setup_requirements: Final demo reel video file (MP4, 1080p minimum)
Presentation notes for technical Q&A
Peer evaluation forms
Course evaluation survey

review.value: Specific feedback that identifies what works, what could improve, and how. 'The footstep layering is effective but the low-end mud between 200-400 Hz reduces impact clarity' is useful; 'sounds good' is not.
Using GPU ray tracing to simulate sound propagation, reflection, and diffraction in real time based on actual scene geometry rather than pre-baked approximations.
A format where individual sounds are positioned in 3D space as objects rather than assigned to speaker channels, allowing the playback system to render them optimally for any speaker configuration.

A structured reflection on a completed project covering what went well, what went wrong, and what you would do differently. Essential for professional growth and commonly presented at industry conferences.

quizzes.topic: Audio Fundamentals, Foley, Synthesis, and Adaptive Music

questions.question: A game requires footstep sounds for a character walking on gravel. Describe a Foley recording approach and explain how you would process the raw recordings to make them game-ready, including at least three specific processing steps.
questions.answer: Record multiple footstep performances on real gravel using a close-miked shotgun or cardioid microphone in a quiet environment. Processing steps: (1) trim and normalize each step to consistent peak levels, (2) apply high-pass filtering around 80-100 Hz to remove low-frequency rumble and room noise, (3) add subtle EQ boost in the 2-5 kHz range to enhance the crunch and detail of the gravel, (4) apply light compression to tame transient peaks while preserving dynamics, and (5) export as individual one-shot samples at 48 kHz / 16-bit for middleware integration.

rubric.key: Describes appropriate Foley recording technique with microphone choice
Lists at least three specific processing steps with reasoning
Mentions game-ready export considerations (format, sample rate, naming)

questions.question: Explain the difference between horizontal re-sequencing and vertical remixing in adaptive music systems. Give a concrete gameplay example where each technique would be most appropriate.
questions.answer: Horizontal re-sequencing switches between different musical sections at defined transition points -- for example, transitioning from an exploration melody to a combat theme when enemies are detected, with the switch occurring at the next bar boundary. Vertical remixing adds or removes instrument layers within the same musical piece -- for example, gradually adding percussion, bass, and brass layers as the player progresses deeper into a dungeon, increasing tension without changing the harmonic structure. Horizontal is best for distinct state changes (peaceful to combat), while vertical is best for gradual intensity shifts within a single state.

rubric.key: Correctly defines horizontal re-sequencing with transition mechanism
Correctly defines vertical remixing with layering mechanism
Provides appropriate and distinct gameplay examples for each

questions.question: What is the difference between subtractive synthesis and FM synthesis? Describe a type of game sound effect that each technique is best suited for and explain why.
questions.answer: Subtractive synthesis starts with harmonically rich oscillators (sawtooth, square wave) and shapes the sound by filtering out frequencies, making it ideal for organic, warm sounds like engine hums, wind effects, or bass impacts where you sculpt from a broad spectrum. FM synthesis uses one oscillator (modulator) to modulate the frequency of another (carrier), producing complex harmonic spectra well-suited for metallic, glassy, or bell-like sounds -- ideal for sci-fi UI elements, magical spell effects, or crystalline impact sounds where inharmonic overtones create otherworldly character.

rubric.key: Accurately explains subtractive synthesis mechanism
Accurately explains FM synthesis mechanism
Identifies appropriate sound types for each with reasoning

quizzes.topic: Spatial Audio, Recording Techniques, and 3D Sound

questions.question: A player is standing in a stone corridor. An explosion occurs in a room behind a closed wooden door. Describe how an audio engine should process this sound differently than if the door were open, referencing specific spatial audio concepts.
questions.answer: With the door closed, the audio engine should apply occlusion filtering: a low-pass filter attenuates high frequencies (above approximately 1-2 kHz) because the wooden door absorbs and blocks higher-frequency sound waves while allowing lower frequencies to pass through. The direct path volume should be reduced based on the door material's transmission loss value. Additionally, the engine should route some signal through a muffled reverb to simulate sound traveling through the door material. When the door is open, the full-spectrum direct sound reaches the player with normal distance attenuation, and the reverb characteristics of both the corridor and the source room blend naturally.

rubric.key: Correctly applies occlusion concept with frequency filtering
Explains material-based transmission loss affecting volume and spectrum
Contrasts open vs closed door audio path differences

questions.question: You are recording ambient forest sounds for an open-world RPG. Describe your microphone setup, recording strategy, and at least two post-production steps to create a seamlessly looping ambient bed suitable for real-time playback.
questions.answer: Use a matched stereo pair of condenser microphones in an ORTF or spaced pair configuration to capture a natural stereo image of the forest. Record at 48 kHz / 24-bit for at least 5-10 minutes continuously to capture natural variation in bird calls, wind, and insect activity. Choose a time of day with consistent ambience and minimal human noise. Post-production: (1) select the cleanest 2-3 minute section and apply gentle noise reduction to remove any low-frequency rumble or intermittent unwanted sounds, (2) create a seamless loop by crossfading the end into the beginning over 2-4 seconds, ensuring no rhythmic patterns (like a repeating bird call) are audible at the loop point, and verify the loop is inaudible when tested at normal playback volume.

rubric.key: Appropriate stereo microphone setup for ambient recording
Sound recording strategy with format and duration considerations
At least two specific post-production steps including loop creation

questions.question: Explain what HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) is and why it is important for game audio. Under what playback conditions does HRTF work correctly, and when does it fail?
questions.answer: HRTF is a mathematical model of how sound is filtered by the shape of the human outer ear (pinna), head, and torso before reaching the eardrums. It encodes direction-dependent frequency changes and inter-aural time/level differences that the brain uses to perceive sound position in 3D space. HRTF works correctly over headphones because the left and right ear signals are isolated -- each ear receives only its intended signal. It fails over speakers because the left speaker's sound reaches the right ear (and vice versa), destroying the inter-aural isolation that HRTF depends on. Additionally, generic HRTFs may not match a specific listener's ear shape, causing front-back confusion or poor elevation perception.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of HRTF and its perceptual function
Correctly identifies headphones as the required playback condition
Explains why HRTF fails on speakers (crosstalk) and with generic profiles

quizzes.topic: Middleware Architecture, Dialogue Systems, and Procedural Audio

questions.question: In FMOD Studio, you are designing a vehicle engine sound system. The game exposes two parameters: engine RPM (0-8000) and throttle load (0.0-1.0). Describe how you would structure the FMOD event to produce a realistic, continuously varying engine sound.
questions.answer: Create a multi-instrument event with RPM mapped to a continuous game parameter on a parameter sheet. Record or source engine samples at multiple RPM points (idle 800, 2000, 4000, 6000, 8000 RPM) for both on-throttle and off-throttle states. Place these as loop regions with RPM as the parameter axis and crossfade regions between adjacent samples for smooth blending. Map throttle load to control crossfade between on-throttle (aggressive, loaded) and off-throttle (deceleration, coasting) sample sets. Add a pitch modulator linked to RPM for fine interpolation between sample points. Layer exhaust pop one-shots triggered randomly during rapid throttle-off transitions.

rubric.key: Describes multi-sample RPM crossfade structure with specific RPM points
Explains throttle load parameter usage for tonal variation between states
Mentions pitch modulation, layering, or transition handling details

questions.question: Compare procedural audio with sample-based audio for a rain system in an open-world game. List two advantages and two disadvantages of the procedural approach, and describe a hybrid strategy that combines both methods.
questions.answer: Procedural advantages: (1) infinite variation without repetition since rain intensity, drop density, and surface impact sounds are generated algorithmically with continuous parameter control, and (2) minimal memory usage since no large ambient loop files need to be stored. Procedural disadvantages: (1) higher CPU cost for real-time synthesis, especially on mobile hardware, and (2) difficulty achieving the organic, natural quality of real recorded rain without extensive tuning. Hybrid strategy: use a procedural noise-based system for the continuous rain bed (filtered noise shaped by intensity parameter), but layer in sample-based one-shot drops for close impacts on surfaces near the camera. The procedural layer provides seamless variation and parameter response, while the samples add realistic transient detail that synthesis struggles to replicate.

rubric.key: Two valid advantages of procedural audio for rain
Two valid disadvantages of procedural audio for rain
Describes a concrete hybrid strategy combining both approaches

questions.question: A game has three simultaneous dialogue requests: a critical story line from the main quest NPC, a merchant's greeting bark, and a companion's combat callout. Describe how a priority-based dialogue system should handle this situation.
questions.answer: The system should assign priority levels: critical story dialogue gets highest priority (P1), combat callout gets medium priority (P2), merchant bark gets lowest priority (P3). The story line plays immediately, triggering dialogue ducking on music and ambience. The combat callout is queued to play after the story line finishes (or after a timeout if the story line is long). The merchant bark is discarded entirely since by the time both higher-priority lines finish, the player will likely have moved away from the merchant. The system should also prevent new bark-level dialogue from interrupting active story or combat dialogue.

rubric.key: Correct priority assignment with clear reasoning
Appropriate handling for each request (play, queue, discard)
Mentions ducking or interruption rules

questions.question: You are composing adaptive music for an RPG that has exploration, tension, and boss battle states. Describe how you would structure your musical material to support seamless transitions between all three states using a combination of horizontal and vertical techniques.
questions.answer: Write all three states in the same key and tempo (or harmonically compatible keys) to enable seamless transitions. Exploration: light instrumentation (acoustic guitar, soft strings, flute) as the base layer. Tension: add a rhythmic percussion layer and shift the harmonic content to minor mode while keeping the same chord roots. Boss battle: full orchestration with aggressive percussion, brass, and distorted bass. Use vertical remixing between exploration and tension (adding/removing layers), and horizontal re-sequencing from tension to boss battle (switching to a new section at the next bar boundary with a stinger transition). The exploration layer remains as a harmonic foundation throughout, with layers added on top for intensity.

rubric.key: Shared key/tempo for transition coherence
Clear description of instrumentation per state with escalating intensity
Appropriate use of both vertical and horizontal techniques with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the purpose of a mix bus hierarchy in game audio and describe a bus structure appropriate for an action RPG with music, dialogue, ambient sounds, combat SFX, and UI sounds. Include at least one example of a dynamic mixing rule.
questions.answer: A mix bus hierarchy groups related sounds into controllable routing groups, allowing independent volume, EQ, and effects processing per category. Structure: Master bus receives all audio. Under it: Music bus (sub-buses for exploration, combat, stingers), Dialogue bus (sub-buses for story, barks, narration), SFX bus (sub-buses for combat, movement, environment), Ambience bus (sub-buses for weather, wildlife, room tone), and UI bus. Dynamic mixing rule: when the Dialogue bus signal exceeds -20 dB, apply sidechain ducking to the Music bus reducing it by -8 dB with 50ms attack and 500ms release, ensuring speech is always intelligible over the score.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of bus hierarchy purpose and benefit
Appropriate bus structure with logical groupings and sub-buses
Specific dynamic mixing rule with concrete parameter values

questions.question: What is a mix snapshot in game audio middleware, and describe three different game states that would each require a distinct mix snapshot with specific mixing differences between them.
questions.answer: A mix snapshot is a saved mixer state that defines volume levels, effects settings, and bus processing for a specific game context, with smooth transitions between snapshots. Three states: (1) Gameplay: all buses at nominal levels, full spatial audio processing, music at -6 dB relative to SFX for action clarity. (2) Pause menu: music fades to -12 dB and applies a low-pass filter at 2 kHz for a muffled effect, all SFX and ambience muted, UI bus becomes primary, transition time 300ms. (3) Cutscene: dialogue bus boosted +3 dB, music crossfaded to cutscene score, SFX reduced to cinematic levels, spatial audio disabled for pre-mixed cinematic audio, transition time 500ms.

rubric.key: Correct definition of mix snapshot with transition concept
Three distinct game states identified
Specific mixing differences for each state with parameter values

quizzes.topic: Optimization, Genre Audio, and Professional Practices

questions.question: A mobile game is exceeding its audio memory budget of 32 MB. The current audio assets total 48 MB: 20 MB music, 15 MB SFX, 8 MB dialogue, 5 MB ambience. Describe a specific optimization strategy for each category to bring the total under budget while preserving perceived audio quality.
questions.answer: Music (20 MB -> 8 MB): Convert from Vorbis quality 8 to quality 4 (roughly 50% size reduction) and stream all music from disk instead of loading into memory, reducing the in-memory footprint to only the streaming buffer (~1-2 MB). SFX (15 MB -> 12 MB): Identify duplicate or near-duplicate sounds and consolidate variations. Convert long SFX (> 2 seconds) to Vorbis and keep short SFX (< 0.5 seconds) as ADPCM. Reduce sample rate of non-critical SFX from 48 kHz to 22.05 kHz. Dialogue (8 MB -> 5 MB): Stream all dialogue from disk (like music) reducing memory to buffer size. Use lower bitrate Vorbis encoding since speech tolerates more compression than music. Ambience (5 MB -> 3 MB): Stream ambient loops from disk and use shorter loop lengths with crossfade points. Total estimated: ~22 MB in-memory.

rubric.key: Specific optimization strategy for each of the four categories
Strategies are technically sound and would achieve the budget target
Considers quality trade-offs and justifies decisions

questions.question: Compare the audio design requirements of a competitive first-person shooter and a narrative horror game. For each genre, identify the single most important audio system and explain why it takes priority over other audio elements.
questions.answer: Competitive FPS: The most important system is precise spatial audio positioning. In competitive play, the ability to locate enemies by sound (footsteps, weapon fire, reloading) directly determines match outcomes. Players use directional audio cues for tactical decisions before visual confirmation. This requires HRTF-accurate spatialization, material-based footstep differentiation, and consistent distance-based attenuation. It takes priority because gameplay fairness and competitive integrity depend on it. Narrative Horror: The most important system is dynamic silence and tension management through adaptive ambience. Horror depends on psychological manipulation through what you do not hear as much as what you do. Sustained low-frequency drones, sudden silence after established ambient sound, and subtle environmental audio that suggests unseen threats create anxiety. This takes priority because the core emotional experience of fear requires audio to lead the player's psychological state.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies spatial audio as FPS priority with gameplay reasoning
Correctly identifies tension/silence management as horror priority with emotional reasoning
Clear contrast between the two approaches and their justifications

questions.question: What are the three most important elements of a game audio demo reel, and what is the single biggest mistake students make when creating their first demo reel?
questions.answer: Three most important elements: (1) Before/after comparison showing your audio redesign against original or silent gameplay, which is the clearest way to demonstrate the value and quality of your work. (2) Strong opening -- the first 15-30 seconds must showcase your best, most polished work because hiring managers will stop watching if the opening is weak. (3) Clear labeling of your specific contribution to each piece, especially for collaborative projects. The single biggest mistake is making the reel too long. Students include everything they have ever made instead of curating the 2-3 best pieces. A 5-minute reel with mediocre work mixed in damages your perceived skill level more than a 90-second reel with only excellent work. Your weakest piece defines the hiring manager's impression.

rubric.key: Identifies three valid important elements with explanation
Identifies a genuine common mistake with clear reasoning
Demonstrates understanding of hiring manager perspective

major_assignments.title: Complete Sound Design Package for a Game Level
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete audio package for a provided game level prototype. This includes environmental ambience, character sound effects, interactive object sounds, UI audio feedback, and an adaptive music system that responds to at least two gameplay parameters. All audio must be original work created using Foley recording, synthesis, or a combination of both.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency in Foley recording and synthesis for game SFX creation
Build an adaptive music system using horizontal and vertical techniques in FMOD
Implement spatial audio with proper attenuation, occlusion, and reverb zones
Integrate all audio through FMOD Studio with professional event architecture and parameter mapping
deliverables: FMOD Studio project with all events, parameters, buses, and banks organized professionally
Unity project with integrated audio playing correctly in-game across all gameplay scenarios
Audio asset library with source files, processing chains documented, and game-ready exports
Technical document describing the audio architecture, parameter mappings, and design rationale (4-5 pages)

criteria: Original sound effects are well-crafted and appropriate for the game context
Variety and randomization prevent repetition fatigue across extended play sessions
Audio assets are technically clean with no clicks, pops, or unwanted noise artifacts
Consistent loudness and tonal quality across the entire asset library
Music transitions smoothly between states without audible glitches or timing errors
At least two gameplay parameters drive meaningful musical changes
Musical content is compositionally coherent across all adaptive states
Transition timing feels natural and responsive to gameplay events
3D sounds are properly spatialized with appropriate distance attenuation curves
Occlusion and obstruction filtering are correctly applied for geometry-blocked sounds
Reverb zones match the acoustic properties of each environment in the level
Listener and emitter positions update correctly during all gameplay movement
Clear description of audio architecture decisions with reasoning
Parameter mapping tables for all interactive audio events
Asset list with source descriptions, processing chains, and file specifications
Professional formatting and technical writing quality
Comprehensive implementation covering all audio categories in the game level
Exceptional sound design quality demonstrating creative range and technical polish
Seamless adaptive systems that enhance gameplay immersion
Professional-level documentation suitable for a studio production pipeline

major_assignments.title: Middleware Integration and Dynamic Audio Systems
major_assignments.description: Build a complete middleware audio integration for a provided game prototype featuring: a surface-based footstep system, velocity-scaled impact sounds, a dialogue priority system with ducking, and a weather audio system driven by a single intensity parameter. All systems must be implemented through FMOD with Unity integration code demonstrating professional programming patterns.
objectives: Implement game-driven audio systems using FMOD Studio events and game parameters
Write clean, maintainable audio integration code following the audio manager pattern
Design middleware events with appropriate randomization, parameter mapping, and mix routing
Profile and optimize audio performance to meet target platform budgets
deliverables: FMOD Studio project with all four audio systems (footsteps, impacts, dialogue, weather) fully configured
Unity project with integration scripts demonstrating audio manager pattern and parameter control
Performance profiling report showing voice count, CPU usage, and memory allocation
Technical documentation with system diagrams, API reference, and integration guide (3-4 pages)

criteria: Footstep system correctly detects surface type and selects appropriate sounds with randomized variation
Impact system scales volume and selects variants based on collision velocity with appropriate thresholds
Dialogue priority system handles simultaneous requests with correct queuing and interruption behavior
Weather system smoothly transitions between clear, rain, and storm states driven by intensity parameter
Audio manager provides clean API that hides middleware complexity from gameplay code
Event instances are properly managed with correct lifecycle (create, play, release)
Code is well-structured with separation of concerns between audio systems
Error handling prevents audio failures from crashing the game
FMOD events use parameters, randomization, and automation appropriately
Bus routing and mix snapshots are configured for correct audio grouping
Sound banks are organized logically for efficient loading
Profiling shows audio CPU under 5% and voice count under 64
System architecture diagrams clearly communicate the design
Integration guide is detailed enough for another programmer to work with the system
Profiling results are presented with analysis and optimization decisions explained
Professional writing quality and technical accuracy
Four complete audio systems functioning correctly in the game prototype
Professional code architecture demonstrating production-ready patterns
Well-designed middleware events with appropriate parameter-driven behavior
Thorough documentation and profiling demonstrating technical competence

major_assignments.title: Original Game Audio Demo Reel
major_assignments.description: Create a professional demo reel demonstrating mastery of game audio by redesigning the complete audio for a provided game prototype or a short game of your own creation. The reel must showcase original sound design, adaptive music, spatial audio, and middleware integration. This is a portfolio-quality capstone project.
objectives: Create portfolio-ready game audio content demonstrating professional-level skills across all audio disciplines
Produce a demo reel video that effectively communicates your abilities to industry hiring managers
Deliver a complete middleware project with production-quality architecture and documentation
Reflect on the creative process through a post-mortem analysis
deliverables: Demo reel video (3-5 minutes, MP4, 1080p minimum) with before/after comparisons
Final game build with fully integrated audio across all systems
FMOD or Wwise project with all source assets and professional event architecture
Audio design document and technical breakdown (5-6 pages)
Post-mortem report analyzing what worked, what was challenging, and lessons learned (2-3 pages)

criteria: Original, high-quality sound effects appropriate for the game context and genre
Creative use of both Foley recording and synthesis techniques demonstrating range
Sufficient variety and randomization to prevent repetition across extended gameplay
Technical quality is artifact-free with consistent loudness and tonal character
Original musical compositions that enhance gameplay atmosphere and emotional impact
Smooth adaptive transitions driven by gameplay parameters with no audible glitches
Musical coherence across all states, layers, and transitions
Professional production quality in arrangement, recording, and mixing
Clean middleware project architecture with logical event organization and naming
Correct spatial audio implementation with attenuation, occlusion, and reverb
Efficient resource usage within reasonable CPU and memory budgets
Bug-free audio playback across all gameplay scenarios
Demo reel is engaging and clearly showcases skills within the first 30 seconds
Before/after comparisons effectively demonstrate the value of the audio work
Documentation is thorough and professionally written
Post-mortem demonstrates reflective learning and professional self-assessment
Portfolio-quality demo reel demonstrating mastery across game audio disciplines
Complete audio redesign with original sound design, adaptive music, and spatial implementation
Professional middleware architecture and bug-free game integration
Thorough documentation and reflective post-mortem analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing audio theory, middleware knowledge, spatial audio concepts, and professional practices

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on recording, synthesis, and middleware exercises completed during lab sessions

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive audio design projects demonstrating integrated creative and technical skills

midterm_examination.description: Mixed format exam: listening analysis, technical questions, and practical sound design exercise

final_project.description: Demo reel capstone project showcasing all course competencies in a portfolio-ready format

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions, recording sessions, and project presentations. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. Recording session deadlines are firm due to studio scheduling.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. Audio assets must be original work -- no royalty-free library sounds in graded submissions unless explicitly permitted. Peer feedback during critiques is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audio content must be original or properly attributed. Use of AI-generated audio must be disclosed and approved by the instructor. Plagiarism or uncredited sample use will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a DAW (Reaper recommended), FMOD Studio, studio headphones, and a recording device. Lab studio equipment is available during scheduled hours.
